title: Ras el hanout
subtitle: Moroccan spice blend
excerpt: The master spice blend of Moroccan cooking, mixed from cumin, coriander, ginger, cinnamon, turmeric, paprika, cardamom, allspice, black pepper, cayenne, and nutmeg. Use it in tagines, as a dry rub for lamb, or stirred into couscous broth. Making it at home takes 5 minutes and keeps for 6 months.
sourceNotes: Ras el hanout (meaning 'head of the shop' in Arabic — the best the spice merchant has to offer) is a variable blend with no single fixed recipe. Commercial versions contain anywhere from 12 to 30 spices. The home version below contains the consistent core spices found in most Moroccan kitchens. Paula Wolfert documents traditional versions with dried rosebuds, Spanish fly, and monk's pepper; this recipe uses the standard daily cooking version.

recipe.batchNotes: Store in an airtight jar away from light. Keeps for 6 months.

content.text: The proportions here are a starting point. Moroccan cooks adjust ras el hanout to taste and to the dish it will be used in: more cinnamon for lamb tagines, more cumin for fish, more cayenne for a hotter blend. The recipe below is the all-purpose version. Toasting the whole spices before grinding gives a deeper, rounder flavour than using pre-ground — but pre-ground works perfectly well for everyday use.

If making with whole spices: toast cumin seeds, coriander seeds, and cardamom pods in a dry pan until fragrant (2 minutes), then grind with the remaining whole spices. If using all pre-ground spices, simply mix. The result is a warm, earthy, complex blend that is more than the sum of its parts.

Combine all ingredients in a small bowl: {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{turmeric}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, {{black-pepper}}, {{cardamom-ground}}, {{allspice}}, {{cayenne}}, and {{nutmeg}}. Stir until evenly mixed. Transfer to an airtight jar. Taste and adjust: add more cayenne for heat, more cinnamon for warmth, more cumin for depth.

items.symptom: Blend tastes harsh rather than warm
items.cause: Old or low-quality spices, or spices not toasted
items.fix: Use fresh spices — old ground spices lose their volatile oils and develop a dusty, bitter flavour. Toast whole spices before grinding for a noticeably deeper result
Reduce cayenne to a pinch. The heat in ras el hanout should be warm and diffuse, not sharp

items.cause: No cardamom or cardamom too old
items.fix: Cardamom is the spice that gives ras el hanout its distinctively Moroccan character. Use freshly ground pods if possible

content.text: Every Moroccan household has ras el hanout, and every cook's version is slightly different. The blend is the taste of Moroccan cooking in concentrated form: the warm, layered spicing that distinguishes it from other North African cuisines. Use it in lamb tagines, as a dry rub for chicken before roasting, stirred into the couscous broth, or mixed with olive oil as a marinade for fish. A tablespoon added to roasted root vegetables or lentils is the simplest way to introduce Moroccan flavour to non-Moroccan dishes.
